choices: A decoy file in a plausible-looking location, crafted to look attractive to ransomware's file-walking and encryption logic. Legitimate users and applications generally don't touch it.

choices.label: A real customer data file that Huntress monitors for any access.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Real customer files aren't the canaries; decoys are. The whole point is that legitimate users have no reason to touch them.

choices.label: A scheduled scan that detects ransomware signatures.
choices.rationale: That's signature-based AV. Canaries are tripwires, not scanners.

choices.label: An email-based alert to the customer when ransomware is suspected.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the mechanic. Canaries are filesystem decoys.

questions.explanation: The agent watches for the touch pattern; the SOC raises Critical when it fires.

questions.prompt: Where do canaries sit in Huntress's product structure?
choices: Inside Managed EDR. The same agent that runs EDR places and watches the canaries; they're an EDR feature, not a separate product surface.

choices.label: A fourth product surface alongside EDR, ITDR, and SIEM.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the product structure. Huntress is three surfaces and canaries ride inside EDR.

choices.label: Inside ITDR, because they detect attacker activity.
choices.rationale: Wrong. ITDR is the identity surface; canaries are filesystem-based and live with EDR.

choices.label: A standalone product the customer enables separately.
choices.rationale: Wrong. They're enabled with the EDR agent, not separately.

questions.explanation: The temptation to round Huntress up to four surfaces is wrong on the product structure. Canaries are counted inside EDR.

questions.prompt: A canary-trip Incident Report lands. What's the reflex per the lesson?
choices: See canary trip, isolate host, escalate in parallel. No reading the Evidence first, no calling the user, no checking whether the trip was 'a real one'.

choices.label: Read the Evidence to confirm the canary trip was a genuine ransomware event before isolating.
choices.rationale: Named common mistake. Second-guessing the SOC applied to the most time-critical incident type.

choices.label: Call the user first to ask what they touched.
choices.rationale: Named common mistake. Isolate first, call after.

choices.label: File an exclusion request to stop similar canary trips from firing in the future.
choices.rationale: Catastrophically wrong on canary trips. Exclusions during or around Criticals are explicitly out of scope.

questions.explanation: The isolation rule applies with extra force on Critical. Isolate first, every time.

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson insist on "don't investigate the canary itself"?
choices: The SOC has already classified the trip as real when they raised it. By the time you've finished a tech-seat investigation, the attacker has had the same minutes to keep encrypting on other hosts.

choices.label: Investigation is forbidden by Huntress licensing terms.
choices.rationale: Fabricated licensing claim. The reason is operational, not contractual.

choices.label: Investigation would alert the attacker to the agent's presence.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's reason.

choices.label: Investigation is fine as long as the tech is documenting as they go.
choices.rationale: Documentation doesn't change the scope rule.

questions.explanation: Containment closes the time window. Investigation extends it.

questions.prompt: 03:14 Saturday, on-call rotation. A Critical lands: customer Northwind Logistics, file server SRV-NORTHWIND-FILE-01, title "Ransomware canary tripped." Recommendation: isolate, preserve evidence, escalate to incident response. What's your first action?

choices.label: Read the Evidence to confirm it's a real ransomware event before isolating.
choices.rationale: Worst response on the most time-critical incident type.

choices: Isolate SRV-NORTHWIND-FILE-01 via the portal, then call the on-call senior on the runbook's dedicated number, then start the PSA entry.

choices.label: Call the customer's IT manager first to let them know what's happening.
choices.rationale: Wrong order. Customer call comes after containment and senior awareness.

choices.label: Reply to the SOC asking whether the trip is genuine before acting.
choices.rationale: Same family as the Evidence-first option. The SOC has classified; you act.

questions.explanation: Containment in under 60 seconds, senior notification in parallel. Documentation immediately after but doesn't delay containment.

questions.prompt: You isolated the file server. Senior is on the phone. They said "don't touch anything else on Northwind's portal, I'm going to look at the network-level scope." Two minutes later a second Critical canary trip lands on SRV-NORTHWIND-APP-02. What do you do?
choices: Isolate SRV-NORTHWIND-APP-02 immediately, confirming with the senior in real time as you do it: "second canary just fired on SRV-NORTHWIND-APP-02, isolating now."

choices.label: Wait for the senior to explicitly tell you to isolate this one too, they said 'don't touch anything else'.
choices.rationale: Misreads the senior's direction. 'Don't touch anything else' was about not freelancing outside the containment workflow; a new Critical canary IS the containment workflow.

choices.label: Ask the senior whether to also call the customer's IT manager now that there's a second canary.
choices.rationale: Wrong timing. Containment of the new host is the next action, not customer comms.

choices.label: Wait until the senior pauses their network-scope review to update them before isolating.
choices.rationale: Same shape as waiting for permission. The reflex doesn't pause for per-host permission.

questions.explanation: The reflex doesn't pause for per-host permission. Confirm-as-you-act, not ask-then-act.

questions.prompt: The customer's IT contact phones in mid-response: "Un-isolate that file server now, we're sure that canary trip was a mistake." What do you do?
choices: Tell the customer the isolation stays until cleared by the senior leading the response, not by customer preference. Escalate the request to the senior as a customer-relationship piece.

choices.label: Un-isolate the host, the customer's IT is the authoritative voice on their own environment.
choices.rationale: Wrong. Customer preference doesn't override the SOC's Critical classification.

choices.label: Un-isolate but warn the customer it might re-fire.
choices.rationale: Same family of mistake. The isolation doesn't lift on a customer call regardless of warnings.

choices.label: Tell the customer 'no, can't help' and end the call.
choices.rationale: Wrong tone and wrong process. Acknowledge, hold the line, route up.

questions.explanation: The isolation stays until cleared by the senior, not by the customer.

questions.prompt: Canaries trip before users notice encrypted files, ransom notes, or performance impact, and that's the point of the decoy.
questions.explanation: The canary's value is the early-warning role. By the time a user notices, the encryption is already underway.

questions.prompt: Per the lesson's procedure, which of these is NOT something the tech does after a canary trip?

choices.label: Isolate the host within 30 seconds via the portal.
choices.rationale: Named step. Containment is first.

choices.label: Phone the on-call senior, with chat channel and PSA flag as backup.
choices.rationale: Named step. All three on a Critical isn't excessive.

choices: Read the Evidence end-to-end and write a tech-level summary for the senior.

choices.label: Stand by for senior direction.
choices.rationale: Named step. The senior either takes the response over or directs you on specific support tasks.

questions.explanation: Reading the Evidence to summarise it is the overstep failure. The SOC's Evidence is for the senior to weigh, not the tech to pre-process.

questions.prompt: You isolated the host, but the on-call senior isn't answering, phone to voicemail, no chat response. What does the lesson say to do?
choices: Escalate up the on-call chain per the runbook, don't wait.

choices.label: Wait 15 minutes and try again.
choices.rationale: The waiting cost is the attacker's continuing movement.

choices.label: Start investigating the canary trip yourself in the meantime to be helpful when the senior arrives.
choices.rationale: Overstep. Containment is held; investigation isn't a stand-in for the senior.

choices.label: Un-isolate the host because there's no senior available to drive the response.
choices.rationale: The opposite of the rule. The isolation stays; the senior is escalated past.

questions.explanation: Senior unreachable means up the chain, not loop on the same name.
